knowledges.content: Let $\mathbb{N}$ denote the set of positive integers $\{1, 2, 3, \ldots\}$.
knowledges.label: s_positive_integers
knowledges.title: Positive integers N

knowledges.content: For each $n \in \mathbb{N}$, define $\sigma_n := \{m \in \mathbb{N} : \gcd(n, m) = 1\}$, the set of positive integers coprime to $n$. In particular, $\sigma_1 = \mathbb{N}$.
knowledges.label: s_coprimality_set
knowledges.title: Coprimality sets sigma_n

knowledges.content: Define $\beta := \{\sigma_n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$. The topology $\tau$ on $\mathbb{N}$ is the topology generated by $\beta$ as a base. The topological space is denoted $X := (\mathbb{N}, \tau)$.
knowledges.label: s_base_and_topology
knowledges.title: Base beta and topology tau on N

knowledges.content: Let $P$ denote the set of prime numbers. For any prime $p$, define $M_p := \{kp : k \in \mathbb{N}\}$, the set of all positive multiples of $p$.
knowledges.label: s_primes_and_multiples
knowledges.title: Primes P and multiples M_p

knowledges.content: Define $N_1 := \mathbb{N} \setminus \{1\}$ and equip it with the subspace topology $\tau_1 := \{N_1 \cap O : O \in \tau\}$, generated by the base $\beta_1 := \{N_1 \cap \sigma_n : \sigma_n \in \beta\}$. The topological subspace is denoted $X_1 := (N_1, \tau_1)$.
knowledges.label: s_subspace
knowledges.title: Subspace X_1 = (N_1, tau_1)

knowledges.content: For all positive integers $x, n, m$: $\gcd(x, n) = 1$ and $\gcd(x, m) = 1$ if and only if $\gcd(x, nm) = 1$. As a consequence, $\sigma_{nm} = \sigma_n \cap \sigma_m$ for all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$.
knowledges.label: gcd_multiplicativity
knowledges.title: GCD multiplicativity

knowledges.content: Every integer $n > 1$ can be expressed as a product of prime numbers (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). Equivalently, every integer greater than 1 is divisible by at least one prime, so $\bigcup_{p \in P} M_p = \mathbb{N} \setminus \{1\}$.
knowledges.label: fundamental_theorem_arithmetic
knowledges.title: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic

knowledges.content: In any topological space $(Y, \mathcal{T})$, for any collection of subsets $\{A_\alpha\}$, the union of closures is contained in the closure of the union: $\bigcup_{\alpha} \operatorname{cl}_Y(A_\alpha) \subseteq \operatorname{cl}_Y\!\left(\bigcup_{\alpha} A_\alpha\right)$.
knowledges.label: closure_union_containment
knowledges.title: Union of closures is contained in closure of union

knowledges.content: In topological spaces, density is transitive: if $A$ is dense in subspace $B$ and $B$ is dense in ambient space $C$, then $A$ is dense in $C$.
knowledges.label: density_transitivity
knowledges.title: Transitivity of density

knowledges.content: $\beta = \{\sigma_n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$ is a base for some topology on $\mathbb{N}$. This holds because (1) $\bigcup_{n \in \mathbb{N}} \sigma_n = \sigma_1 = \mathbb{N}$, and (2) for all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$, $\sigma_{nm} = \sigma_n \cap \sigma_m \in \beta$, so the intersection of any two base elements contains a base element.
knowledges.label: thm_base
knowledges.title: Theorem 0.1: beta is a topological base

knowledges.content: For every positive integer $n$, the set $\sigma_n = \{m \in \mathbb{N} : \gcd(n, m) = 1\}$ is infinite. For $n > 1$, $\sigma_n$ consists of all positive integers in the residue classes $m \pmod{n}$ with $\gcd(m, n) = 1$; each such arithmetic progression $\{m, m+n, m+2n, \ldots\}$ is infinite.
knowledges.label: sigma_n_infinite
knowledges.title: Remark 0.2: sigma_n is infinite

knowledges.content: The topology $\tau$ on $\mathbb{N}$ generated by $\beta = \{\sigma_n\}$ is strictly coarser than Golomb's topology on $\mathbb{N}$ (which uses arithmetic progressions $\{a + nd : n \geq 0\}$ with $\gcd(a, d) = 1$ as a base).
knowledges.label: tau_coarser_golomb
knowledges.title: Remark 0.2: tau is strictly coarser than Golomb's topology

knowledges.content: The topological space $X = (\mathbb{N}, \tau)$ does not satisfy the $T_0$ separation axiom: there exist distinct points in $\mathbb{N}$ that cannot be topologically distinguished by any open set in $\tau$.
knowledges.label: x_not_t0
knowledges.title: X is not T_0

knowledges.content: The topological space $X = (\mathbb{N}, \tau)$ is hyperconnected: every two non-empty open sets in $\tau$ have non-empty intersection.
knowledges.label: x_hyperconnected
knowledges.title: X is hyperconnected

knowledges.content: The topological space $X = (\mathbb{N}, \tau)$ is ultraconnected: every two non-empty closed sets in $\tau$ have non-empty intersection.
knowledges.label: x_ultraconnected
knowledges.title: X is ultraconnected

knowledges.content:
If $p$ is a prime number, then $\operatorname{cl}_X(\{p\}) = M_p$ in the topological space $X = (\mathbb{N}, \tau)$, where $M_p$ is the set of all positive multiples of $p$ and $\operatorname{cl}_X$ denotes closure in $X$.

**Proof sketch:** ($\subseteq$) If $x \in \operatorname{cl}_X(\{p\})$ and $x \notin M_p$, then $\gcd(x, p) = 1$, so $x \in \sigma_p$. But then $p \in \sigma_p$ would require $\gcd(p, p) = 1$, a contradiction since $\gcd(p, p) = p \geq 2$. ($\supseteq$) If $x = np \in M_p$ and $\sigma_k$ is any basic open set containing $x$, then $\gcd(np, k) = 1$ implies $\gcd(p, k) = 1$, so $p \in \sigma_k$. Hence $x \in \operatorname{cl}_X(\{p\})$.
knowledges.label: lem_closure_prime
knowledges.title: Lemma 0.4: cl_X({p}) = M_p

knowledges.content: For every integer $n > 1$, $\operatorname{cl}_X(\{n\}) = \bigcap_{p \mid n} M_p$ in the topological space $X = (\mathbb{N}, \tau)$, where the intersection ranges over all prime divisors $p$ of $n$.
knowledges.label: thm_closure_n
knowledges.title: Theorem 0.5: cl_X({n}) = intersection of M_p over prime divisors of n

knowledges.content: $\operatorname{cl}_X(\{1\}) = \mathbb{N}$, since $\sigma_1 = \mathbb{N}$ implies that 1 belongs to every basic open set, so every point of $\mathbb{N}$ is in the closure of $\{1\}$. Moreover, for every integer $n > 1$, $1 \notin \operatorname{cl}_X(\{n\})$, because $1 \in \sigma_n$ (since $\gcd(1, n) = 1$) while $n \notin \sigma_n$ (since $\gcd(n, n) = n > 1$), so $\sigma_n$ is an open neighborhood of 1 that does not contain $n$.
knowledges.label: remark_closure_1
knowledges.title: Remark 0.6: cl_X({1}) = N and 1 not in cl_X({n})

knowledges.content: The subspace $X_1 = (\mathbb{N} \setminus \{1\}, \tau_1)$ is dense in $X = (\mathbb{N}, \tau)$, i.e., $\operatorname{cl}_X(\mathbb{N} \setminus \{1\}) = \mathbb{N}$. This holds because every non-empty basic open set $\sigma_n$ is infinite (hence contains elements $> 1$), so $\sigma_n \cap (\mathbb{N} \setminus \{1\}) \neq \emptyset$.
knowledges.label: x1_dense_in_x
knowledges.title: X_1 is dense in X

knowledges.content:
$\#P = \aleph_0$ if and only if $P$ is dense in $X = (\mathbb{N}, \tau)$, where $P$ is the set of prime numbers and $\#P$ denotes its cardinality.

($\Rightarrow$) If there are infinitely many primes, then for any $n > 1$, there exists a prime $p > n$, and $\gcd(n, p) = 1$ implies $p \in \sigma_n \cap P$. Since $\sigma_1 = \mathbb{N} \supseteq P$, every basic open set meets $P$.

($\Leftarrow$) If $P$ is dense, let $\{p_1, \ldots, p_k\}$ be any finite collection of primes and set $x = p_1 \cdots p_k$. The basic open set $\sigma_x$ contains no $p_i$ (since $\gcd(p_i, x) = p_i \neq 1$), but density forces $\sigma_x \cap P \neq \emptyset$, producing a prime $q \notin \{p_1, \ldots, p_k\}$. Hence $P$ is infinite.
knowledges.label: thm_density_iff_infinite
knowledges.title: Theorem 0.7: Infinitude of P iff P is dense in X

knowledges.content:
$P$ is dense in $X_1 = (\mathbb{N} \setminus \{1\}, \tau_1)$ if and only if $P$ is dense in $X = (\mathbb{N}, \tau)$.

($\Leftarrow$) If $P$ is dense in $X$, it is clearly dense in any subspace.

($\Rightarrow$) $X_1$ is dense in $X$ (every basic open set $\sigma_n$ is infinite, hence meets $\mathbb{N} \setminus \{1\}$). So by transitivity of density, $P$ dense in $X_1$ and $X_1$ dense in $X$ imply $P$ dense in $X$.
knowledges.label: lem_density_equivalence
knowledges.title: Lemma 0.8: P dense in X_1 iff P dense in X

knowledges.content:
$P$ is dense in $X_1 = (\mathbb{N} \setminus \{1\}, \tau_1)$, i.e., $\operatorname{cl}_{X_1}(P) = N_1$.

The proof combines three ingredients:

**(0.10)** By a standard topological fact, $\bigcup_{p \in P} \operatorname{cl}_{X_1}(\{p\}) \subseteq \operatorname{cl}_{X_1}(P) \subseteq N_1$.

**(0.11)** By Lemma 0.4, $\operatorname{cl}_X(\{p\}) = M_p$ for each prime $p$, so $\bigcup_{p \in P} \operatorname{cl}_{X_1}(\{p\}) = \bigcup_{p \in P} (M_p \cap N_1) = \bigcup_{p \in P} M_p$ (since $M_p \subseteq N_1$ for all primes $p \geq 2$).

**(0.12)** By the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, $\bigcup_{p \in P} M_p = N_1$.

Chaining: $N_1 = \bigcup_{p \in P} M_p = \bigcup_{p \in P} \operatorname{cl}_{X_1}(\{p\}) \subseteq \operatorname{cl}_{X_1}(P) \subseteq N_1$, hence $\operatorname{cl}_{X_1}(P) = N_1$.
knowledges.label: thm_p_dense_x1
knowledges.title: Theorem 0.9: P is dense in X_1

knowledges.content: There are infinitely many prime numbers, i.e., $\#P = \aleph_0$.
knowledges.label: thm_infinitude_primes
knowledges.title: Theorem 0.13: Infinitude of prime numbers

knowledges.content:
Let $A \subseteq P$ be a non-empty subset of primes. Then $A$ is dense in $X = (\mathbb{N}, \tau)$ if and only if $\#A = \aleph_0$. The proof follows the same argument as Theorem 0.7 with $P$ replaced by $A$:

($\Rightarrow$) If $\#A = \aleph_0$, for any $n > 1$ there exists $p \in A$ with $p > n$, so $p \in \sigma_n \cap A$.

($\Leftarrow$) If $A$ is dense, for any finite subset $\{p_1, \ldots, p_k\} \subseteq A$, the set $\sigma_{p_1 \cdots p_k} \cap A$ is non-empty and contains a prime not in $\{p_1, \ldots, p_k\}$.
knowledges.label: thm_subset_dense_iff_infinite
knowledges.title: Theorem 0.14: Subset of primes dense iff infinite

knowledges.content: all_true(github:furstenberg_prime_infinitude::x1_dense_in_x,github:furstenberg_prime_infinitude::density_transitivity)
knowledges.label: __conjunction_result_f6e5ff3d

knowledges.content: all_true(github:furstenberg_prime_infinitude::lem_closure_prime,github:furstenberg_prime_infinitude::fundamental_theorem_arithmetic,github:furstenberg_prime_infinitude::closure_union_containment)
knowledges.label: __conjunction_result_a17b86c0

knowledges.content: all_true(github:furstenberg_prime_infinitude::thm_density_iff_infinite,github:furstenberg_prime_infinitude::lem_density_equivalence,github:furstenberg_prime_infinitude::thm_p_dense_x1)
knowledges.label: __conjunction_result_a4b9fec9

metadata.reason: By @gcd_multiplicativity, $\sigma_{nm} = \sigma_n \cap \sigma_m$ for all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$. This gives condition (2) for a topological base: the intersection of any two base elements $\sigma_n \cap \sigma_m = \sigma_{nm}$ is itself in $\beta$. Condition (1) is immediate: $\bigcup_{n} \sigma_n \supseteq \sigma_1 = \mathbb{N}$. Together, these two conditions establish that $\beta$ is a base for a topology on $\mathbb{N}$.

The proof uses that $\beta$ is a base for $\tau$ (@thm_base), so the basic open sets $\sigma_k$ determine closure in $X$. ($\subseteq$) Suppose $x \in \operatorname{cl}_X(\{p\})$ but $x \notin M_p$. Then $\gcd(x, p) = 1$, so $x \in \sigma_p$. Since $x$ is in the closure of $\{p\}$, every open set containing $x$ must contain $p$, so $p \in \sigma_p$. But $\gcd(p, p) = p \neq 1$, contradiction. ($\supseteq$) If $x = np \in M_p$ and $x \in \sigma_k$, then $\gcd(np, k) = 1$, which forces $\gcd(p, k) = 1$, so $p \in \sigma_k$. Every basic open neighborhood of $x$ contains $p$, hence $x \in \operatorname{cl}_X(\{p\})$.

($\supseteq$) Take $x \in \bigcap_{p|n} M_p$. By @lem_closure_prime, $x \in \operatorname{cl}_X(\{p\})$ for every prime $p | n$. So for every $\sigma_k \ni x$, we have $p \in \sigma_k$ for all $p | n$, meaning $\gcd(p, k) = 1$ for all $p | n$, hence $\gcd(n, k) = 1$, thus $n \in \sigma_k$. Therefore $x \in \operatorname{cl}_X(\{n\})$. ($\subseteq$) Take $x \in \operatorname{cl}_X(\{n\})$ and $\sigma_k \ni x$. Then $n \in \sigma_k$, so $\gcd(n, k) = 1$, which forces $\gcd(p, k) = 1$ for every $p | n$, hence $p \in \sigma_k$. By @lem_closure_prime, $x \in \bigcap_{p|n} M_p$.

Since $\beta$ is a base for $\tau$ (@thm_base), closure is determined by the basic open sets $\sigma_n$. For $\{1\}$: since $\gcd(1, n) = 1$ for all $n$, we have $1 \in \sigma_n$ for all $n$, so $\sigma_1 = \mathbb{N}$ and every basic open set containing any point $m$ also contains $1$ (because $m \in \sigma_k \Rightarrow 1 \in \sigma_k$). Hence $\operatorname{cl}_X(\{1\}) = \mathbb{N}$. For $n > 1$: $1 \in \sigma_n$ but $n \notin \sigma_n$ (since $\gcd(n,n)=n>1$), so $\sigma_n$ separates 1 from $n$, giving $1 \notin \operatorname{cl}_X(\{n\})$.

By @sigma_n_infinite, every $\sigma_n$ is infinite and hence contains elements greater than 1. Therefore every non-empty basic open set in $\tau$ has non-empty intersection with $\mathbb{N} \setminus \{1\} = N_1$. This means $N_1$ meets every open set, so $\operatorname{cl}_X(N_1) = \mathbb{N}$, i.e., $X_1$ is dense in $X$.

Since $ eta$ is a base for $	au$ (@thm_base), density of $P$ in $X$ is equivalent to $P \cap \sigma_n 
eq \emptyset$ for every $\sigma_n \in  eta$. ($\Rightarrow$) If $P$ is infinite, for any $n > 1$ pick a prime $p > n$; then $\gcd(n, p) = 1$ so $p \in \sigma_n \cap P$. For $n = 1$, $\sigma_1 = \mathbb{N} \supseteq P$. ($\Leftarrow$) Given a finite set of primes $\{p_1, \ldots, p_k\}$, set $x = p_1 \cdots p_k$. None of the $p_i$ lie in $\sigma_x$ (since $\gcd(p_i, x) = p_i 
eq 1$). But $P$ is dense, so $\exists q \in P \cap \sigma_x$ with $q 
eq p_i$ for all $i$. Hence $P$ has more than $k$ elements for any $k$.

($\Leftarrow$) Trivial: density in the ambient space implies density in a subspace. ($\Rightarrow$) @x1_dense_in_x establishes that $X_1$ is dense in $X$. If $P$ is dense in $X_1$, then by @density_transitivity (density is transitive), $P$ is dense in $X$.

Step 1 (eq. 0.10): By @closure_union_containment (union of closures $\subseteq$ closure of union), $\bigcup_{p \in P} \operatorname{cl}_{X_1}(\{p\}) \subseteq \operatorname{cl}_{X_1}(P) \subseteq N_1$. Step 2 (eq. 0.11): By @lem_closure_prime, $\operatorname{cl}_X(\{p\}) = M_p$ for each prime $p$, so $\operatorname{cl}_{X_1}(\{p\}) = M_p \cap N_1 = M_p$ (since the smallest element of $M_p$ is $p \geq 2 \in N_1$). Hence $\bigcup_{p \in P} \operatorname{cl}_{X_1}(\{p\}) = \bigcup_{p \in P} M_p$. Step 3 (eq. 0.12): By @fundamental_theorem_arithmetic, every integer $> 1$ has a prime divisor, so $\bigcup_{p \in P} M_p = N_1$. Combining: $N_1 = \bigcup M_p = \bigcup \operatorname{cl}_{X_1}(\{p\}) \subseteq \operatorname{cl}_{X_1}(P) \subseteq N_1$, hence $\operatorname{cl}_{X_1}(P) = N_1$.

By @thm_p_dense_x1, $P$ is dense in $X_1$. By @lem_density_equivalence, $P$ dense in $X_1$ implies $P$ dense in $X$. By the ($\Leftarrow$) direction of @thm_density_iff_infinite, $P$ dense in $X$ implies $\#P = \aleph_0$. Therefore there are infinitely many prime numbers.

The argument is identical to Theorem 0.7, replacing $P$ with an arbitrary non-empty $A \subseteq P$. Since $\beta$ is a base for $\tau$ (@thm_base): ($\Rightarrow$) If $A$ is infinite, for any $n > 1$, pick $p \in A$ with $p > n$; then $\gcd(n, p) = 1$ so $p \in \sigma_n \cap A$, proving density. ($\Leftarrow$) If $A$ is dense, for any finite $\{p_1, \ldots, p_k\} \subseteq A$, set $x = p_1 \cdots p_k$; no $p_i$ lies in $\sigma_x$, but density gives $q \in A \cap \sigma_x$ distinct from all $p_i$, proving $A$ is infinite.
